summary:
The Mughal-Afghan Wars (c. 1556–1761 AD) encompassed the extended struggle between the [[event:103|Mughal Empire]] and various Afghan powers for control of northern India, from the [[event:3234|Sher Shah Suri's]] temporary displacement of the Mughals (1540–1555) through the 18th-century conflicts with the [[event:2451|Durrani Empire]]. The most decisive phase came at the [[event:2448|Third Battle of Panipat]] (1761), when Ahmad Shah Durrani's Afghan forces destroyed the Maratha Confederacy's army — but this came too late to restore Mughal power, which had already been fatally weakened by [[event:2507|Aurangzeb's]] overextension and his successors' civil wars.

The recurring Afghan challenge to Mughal authority reflected the empire's persistent difficulty in controlling the northwest frontier — the corridor through which every conqueror from Alexander the Great to Babur himself had entered India. Afghans served in the Mughal army and administration while also periodically challenging Mughal authority, creating a complex relationship of service and rivalry. After [[event:2447|Nader Shah's]] 1739 sack of Delhi, the Mughal emperor became essentially a figurehead, and the subsequent Durrani invasions (Ahmad Shah crossed the Khyber Pass eight times between 1748 and 1767) reduced northern India to a battleground between Afghan, Maratha, Sikh, and Mughal successor powers. The Mughal-Afghan wars thus form the military backdrop against which the Mughal Empire's decline unfolded and the political vacuum that [[event:2358|British East India Company]] forces would eventually fill.
